content.claim: Hungarian municipal governments historically mitigated efficiency losses from falling pupil numbers by reducing parallel classes rather than closing schools, resulting in a large supply of small schools—a policy trade-off that preserved village institutions at the cost of reduced within-school heterogeneity and segregation effects downstream.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Education finance lens
limitations: Causal claim that small classes increased segregation effects not directly demonstrated with quantitative evidence
No comparison to outcomes under alternative policy (wholesale closure vs. class reduction)
Historical pattern may no longer be operative under centralized governance
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
